title: Mix any colour from three lights
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire an RGB LED — three tiny lights in one dome — to three PWM pins and upload the Arduino sketch. The real payoff is a model you'll use for every screen you own: any colour is three brightnesses — red, green, and blue — each set by its own PWM channel from 0 to 255. The board proves it by mixing colours you never wired by hand.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the RGB LED: a red, a green, and a blue leg each running through its own 220 ohm resistor to a separate GPIO pin, with the long common pin joined to 3.3 volts, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: RGB LED
parts.explanation: An RGB LED is three tiny LEDs — red, green, and blue — in one dome. Set each one's brightness with PWM and your eye blends them into a single colour. All off is dark; all full is white; everything else is a mix — the same trick a screen uses.

parts.name: 3 × 220 Ω resistors
parts.explanation: A resistor limits how much current flows. Placed in series with the LED, the 220 Ω one keeps the current gentle enough to light the LED instead of burning it out. The bands of colour print its value, and it has no direction.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: One PWM channel sets how much red shows.
One PWM channel sets how much green shows.
One PWM channel sets how much blue shows.
All three share this positive pin so a colour lights when its pin goes LOW.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Land the core model- any colour is three brightnesses (red, green, blue), each set by its own PWM channel from 0 to 255, and equal amounts read as grey or white.
Stress that this is a common-anode LED- its shared pin goes to 3.3V, so a colour lights when its pin goes LOW; a common-cathode part shares ground instead and lights on HIGH.
Explain the 255 minus value inversion when asked- it hides the common-anode polarity so a bigger number still means brighter.
For the challenge, have the learner predict the red, green, and blue numbers for orange, purple, and white before setting them, then check the dome against the prediction.

If only one colour appears, check that colour's 220 Ω resistor and GPIO wire before changing code; if nothing lights, check the common pin reaches 3.3V; if a colour is stuck on, suspect common-anode wiring or the wrong leg.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Find the RGB LED's long pin — that's the common one shared by all three colours.
Wire the red leg through a 220 Ω resistor to GPIO 38.
Wire the green leg through a 220 Ω resistor to GPIO 39.
Wire the blue leg through a 220 Ω resistor to GPIO 40.
Run the long common pin to 3.3V, not to ground.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_05.1_RandomColorLight.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Watch the dome change colour again and again.

arduino.explanation: The three pins are the red, green, and blue legs in that order.
Writes 255 minus the value because this common-anode LED lights when its pin goes LOW.
Each colour gets a fresh brightness from 0 to 255, and setting all three at once mixes the shade.

micropython.explanation: MicroPython's duty here is 10-bit, so it inverts with 1023 minus the value instead of 255.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Give red, green, and blue each a level from 0 to 255 and your eye adds the three glows into one colour — the same PWM dimming from Day 7, run on three channels at once.
theoryModel.formula: colour = red + green + blue, each a PWM level from 0 to 255

notes.title: Common anode vs common cathode
notes.body: Three LEDs share one pin. Run that shared pin to 3.3V and you have a common anode, where each colour lights when its own pin goes LOW. Run it to ground and you have a common cathode, lit on HIGH. This kit is a common anode, so the sketch writes 255 minus your value and a bigger number still means brighter.

notes.title: Why the numbers stop at 255
notes.body: The channels are 8-bit, which gives 256 levels per colour, 0 through 255. That is the same ledc hardware from Day 7 doing the fast switching, while your loop just names one level for each of the three colours.

notes.title: Why equal numbers look grey
notes.body: When red, green, and blue are equal, none of them leads, so your eye finds no hue to settle on and reads neutral — grey at a low level, white at a high one.

expectedOutputExample: The RGB LED glows a colour.
Every fraction of a second it jumps to a new random colour.
It keeps changing for as long as the board has power.
test.successCriteria: The colours look random on purpose — each one is a fresh mix of red, green, and blue.

firstChecks: Check that colour's 220 Ω resistor and its GPIO wire — the other two legs may not be reaching their pins.
Check the common pin — it goes to 3.3V, not to ground.
Suspect the common-anode wiring or the wrong leg on that pin.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Predict the red, green, and blue numbers for orange, purple, and white, then replace the three random() calls with your fixed numbers, upload, and see whether the dome matches your prediction.
